title: practical english usage - Part 11
content:
(for example by pronouncing it more loudly; by writing it in capital letters; emphasis  giving special importance to one part of a word or sentence Examples: (It's a) Nice day, isn't it?; It was better than I expected (it would be).

ellipsis  leaving out words when their meaning can be understood from the context.

expression of time to indicate duration.

duration  how  long something lasts. The  preposition for can be used with an frankly; as a matter of fact.

the speaker’ s attitude to what he/she is saying. Examples: on the other hand; example, connect a sentence with what comes before or after, or it may show what is being said and the wider context. A  discourse marker may, for discourse marker  a word or expression which  shows the connection between She looked at me and said, 'This is my money'. See also indirect speech.

speaker (more or less), without any changes of tense, pronouns, etc. Example: direct speech  speech reported 'directly', in the words used by the original Language terminology •  xxiii (e.g. swam, taken); children is an irregular plural.

irregular verb has a past tense and/or past participle that does not end in -ed irregular  not following the normal rules, or not having the usual form. An subject. Examples: Never had she seen such a mess; Here comes John.

inversion  a structure in which  an auxiliary or other verb comes before its in the passive. Examples: smile; fall; come; go.

intransitive  An intransitive verb is one that cannot have an object or be used voice rises and falls to show meaning, sentence structure or mood.

intonation  the 'melody' of spoken language: the way the musical pitch of the What, who and where are interrogative words.

auxiliary be) before the subject (e.g. Can you swim?; Are you ready?).

questions. In an interrogative sentence, there is an auxiliary verb (or non­ interrogative  Interrogative structures and words are used for asking adverbs.

intensifying  making stronger, more emphatic. Very and terribly are intensifying in a sentence. Example: Sometimes I wish I had a different job.

initial  at the beginning. Sometimes is an adverb that can go in initial position running. See also gerund, present participle.

-ing form  the form of a verb ending in -ing. Examples: finding, keeping, is used mostly in an informal style; start is a more informal word than commence.

no special reason to speak politely or carefully. I'll is more informal than I will; get informal  the style used in ordinary conversation, personal letters, etc, when there is business; To err is human,  to forgive divine.

Examples: I want to go home; It's easy to sing; I've got a plan to start a after an adjective or noun, or as the subject or complement of a sentence.

infinitive  the base form of a word (usually with to), used after another verb, reported in indirect speech).

direct speech) and He said that he was tired (the original speaker's words are Compare: He said 'I'm  tired' (the original speaker's words are reported in other words may be different from those used by the original speaker).

it part of our own sentence (so that the tenses, word order, pronouns and indirect speech  a structure in which  we  report what somebody said by making indirect object  see object.

indefinite article  a/an.

Examples: Bring me a pen; Have a good holiday.

imperative  the form of a verb used to give orders, make suggestions, etc.

See also non-identifying relative clause.

to steal your cat identifies the woman - it tells us which  woman is meant.) There's the woman who tried to steal your cat. (The relative clause who tried noun - which  tells us which  person or thing is being talked about. Example: identifying (or defining) relative clause  a relative clause which  identifies a imaginary. Example: What would you do if you had six months free?

hypothetical situations - that is to say, situations which  may not happen, or are hypothetical  Some words and structures (e.g. modal verbs, if-clauses) are used for to show certain kinds of meaning.

grammar  the rules that show how  words are combined, arranged or changed Language terminology •  xxiv relative clause.
